mnemonics.title: Eulerian Path Checklist
mnemonics.content: EVENs start and end DIFFERENT: For path (not circuit), need 2 ODD vertices. ALL EVEN means back to START (circuit). E for Edges, E for Eulerian!

mnemonics.title: Dirac's Degree
mnemonics.content: DIRAC: D=I Require All Connections - Minimum degree n/2 for Hamiltonian. Think 'Half or More' = 'Dirac's Door to Hamiltonian'.

mnemonics.title: Fleury's Bridge Rule
mnemonics.content: FLEURY: First LeaveEdges Understanding Real Yields - Avoid bridges unless forced. Never cross bridges unless you can't!

mnemonics.title: Euler vs Hamiltonian
mnemonics.content: Euler = Every Edge (E); Hamilton = Every Home (vertex) - E for Edges, H for Home/vertices.

mnemonics.title: Degrees Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: Even Edges = Euler Circuit; Odd Odds = Path; No Odds = No Euler. Think: 'Euler Eschews Odd Endpoints!'

acronyms.expansion: Edges Used - Every edge once, Returns to start for circuit
acronyms.usage: For finding trails covering all edges exactly once

acronyms.expansion: Home/vertex - Every vertex once, returns to home for cycle
acronyms.usage: For route problems like TSP and Knight's Tour

acronyms.expansion: Degree Index Requirement for All Connections
acronyms.usage: Sufficient condition: min degree ≥ n/2 for Hamiltonian

visualTips.visualization: Draw vertices as circles with edges as lines. Count lines touching each circle - even degree vertices have paired edges like feet, odd degree vertices have one 'lonely' edge like a single shoe.
Think of drawing a shape without lifting pen - if you end where you started, it's a circuit; if you end somewhere else, it's a path.
Imagine visiting all cities in a country exactly once and returning home - the route forms a cycle through all points.
Draw a graph and imagine the edge is a bridge over water - if you remove it, can you still get across? If not, it's a bridge.
At each step, imagine walking through a room - don't take the 'emergency exit' (bridge) unless it's the only door left.

analogies.analogy: Like a mail carrier delivering mail on every street exactly once - covering all roads (edges) without repetition.
Like a tourist visiting every city in a country exactly once - visiting all destinations (vertices) without repetition.
Like starting from home, delivering mail on every street, and returning home - complete coverage with return.
Like a traveling salesman who must visit every client once and return home - covers all clients, returns to start.
In exploring a cave system, don't take a tunnel that leads to an unexplored chamber unless it's the only way - you might get stuck.
If everyone in a party knows at least half the other guests, everyone can dance in a circle together - the graph is Hamiltonian.
